sections.text: Welcome to Biology, Module Three — Cell Membrane and Transport. Last lesson, you opened the cell and toured its rooms. Today we zoom in on the wall around every cell, and on the only question that really matters about it: what gets in, what gets out, and how the cell decides.

Your blood is salty. The water in a swimming pool is not. So why doesn't your skin dissolve, and why don't your cells explode the moment you jump in? The answer is the cell membrane. It is not a brick wall. It is a checkpoint — a moving, picky, two-layer gate that decides what crosses, when, and at what cost.

Game plan. First, look at the membrane itself — the fluid mosaic model. Second, sort transport into two big buckets — passive, which is free, and active, which costs energy. Third, master osmosis, the special case of water moving across a membrane. Two pauses, one trap, and a real assignment with potato cubes.

The fluid mosaic model. Two layers of phospholipids — heads facing water, tails facing each other. Floating in that sea are proteins. Some open as channels, some pump cargo, some recognize signals. Fluid, because parts drift sideways. Mosaic, because many different pieces are embedded in it. That structure is exactly what makes the membrane selective.

Now sort transport into two buckets. Passive transport — molecules move down their gradient, from high concentration to low. The cell spends zero energy. Three kinds: diffusion, facilitated diffusion, and osmosis. Active transport — molecules move against the gradient, from low to high. That uphill move costs ATP. With the gradient is free. Against the gradient costs money.

Diffusion is any molecule spreading from where it is crowded to where it is not — think perfume in a room. Facilitated diffusion is the same idea, but the molecule is too big or too charged, so a protein channel helps it through. Osmosis is the special case for water. Water moves toward the side with more dissolved stuff. Same logic, different molecule.

Here is the most common mistake. Many students say, water moves from low salt to high salt. They are almost right, but that framing trips them in problems. Say it this way instead. Water moves from where there is more water to where there is less. Hypotonic outside means more water outside — water flows in. Hypertonic outside means less water outside — water flows out.

Pause and try. A piece of potato goes into three cups. Cup A is pure distilled water. Cup B is mild salt water. Cup C is heavy brine. For each cup, predict whether the potato gains mass, loses mass, or stays the same — and say which way water moved across the membranes. Pause now. Write the three predictions. Press play when you are ready.

Answer. Cup A — distilled water — is hypotonic. More water outside than inside the potato, so water moves in. The potato gains mass and feels firm. Cup B is closer to isotonic. Little net change in mass. Cup C — heavy brine — is hypertonic. Less water outside, so water leaves the potato. It loses mass and turns limp. One rule, three predictions.

Now the uphill case. Nerve cells must keep sodium low inside and potassium high inside — both against their natural gradients. The membrane runs a pump that burns one ATP to push three sodium ions out and pull two potassium ions in every cycle. No ATP, no pumping. No pumping, no nerve signal. Active transport keeps conditions that diffusion alone would erase.

Pause and try, round two. Sort each example as passive or active. One — oxygen entering a lung cell. Two — a sodium-potassium pump in a nerve cell. Three — water moving across the wall of the gut. Four — glucose entering a cell through a protein channel. Pause now. Label each one P for passive or A for active. Press play when you are ready.

Answer. One — oxygen entering a lung cell is passive. Small molecule, downhill, no help needed. Two — the sodium-potassium pump is active. It moves ions uphill and burns ATP. Three — water across the gut wall is osmosis, still passive. Four — glucose through a channel is facilitated diffusion, also passive. The protein is not what makes a transport active. The energy cost is.

Recap. Fluid mosaic model — two layers of phospholipids with proteins floating in them. Passive transport flows down a gradient and costs no ATP: diffusion, facilitated diffusion, osmosis. Active transport flows up a gradient and costs ATP. Osmosis follows water — from more water to less. Hypotonic outside, water in, cell swells. Hypertonic outside, water out, cell shrinks. Isotonic, no net change.

How to actually master Module Three. Read OpenStax Biology Chapter Five on membrane structure and function. Run the Khan Academy practice on passive transport. In your Learn Portal dashboard, complete the assignment — design a potato-cube osmosis experiment with a hypothesis, three solutions, a data table, and a conclusion. Book an advisor check-in if the trap still feels fuzzy. Next up — Module Four.
